content:
The hum of the vending machine in the corner isn't a sound anymore; it’s a frequency I feel in my molars. 

I look down at my hands on the desk. The skin looks like the vellum of the 1994 logbook—thin, translucent, mapped with blue veins that mimic the cracked marble of the baseboards. I don't remember when I stopped needing the security monitors to know who was coming through the door. I feel the
